channel.title: Drupal Core
channel.description: Stay informed about developments in Drupal Core.

item.title: #3570235: Deprecate functions in syslog.module
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 4dcc789 · +80/-16 · 4 files
  • Discussion: #3570235 · 4 contributors · 24 comments

Summary

The Syslog module's two remaining procedural functions are now deprecated because they were only used internally by the module itself. The module has been refactored to use modern dependency injection and config targets, making the configuration form simpler and more maintainable. This cleanup removes unnecessary public API surface while keeping all functionality working exactly as before. No contributed modules were found to be using these functions, so the deprecation has no known external impact.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: syslog_facility_list() and syslog_logging_settings_submit() deprecated without replacement; functionality moved to protected methods in SyslogHooks class

Technical details

Two functions in syslog.module have been deprecated: syslog_facility_list() and syslog_logging_settings_submit(). Both were only used internally by the Syslog module to build and process its configuration form.

The functionality has been moved into the SyslogHooks class. The facility list is now a protected facilityList() method called directly from the form alter hook. The submit handler is no longer needed because the form now uses config targets (#config_target) to automatically save values to syslog.settings configuration.

Additional improvements include proper dependency injection of ModuleHandlerInterface into the hooks class, and removal of manual configuration loading and default value setting that config targets handle automatically. The form now relies on the Form API's built-in config target system to handle both populating default values and saving submitted values.

These functions are deprecated without replacement since they were never intended as public API and no known external usage exists.

Upgrade

If you are calling syslog_facility_list() or syslog_logging_settings_submit(), you must update your code. These functions are deprecated in Drupal 11.4.0 and will be removed in Drupal 12.0.0 and 13.0.0 respectively.

There is no direct replacement. If you need a list of syslog facilities, implement your own array:

$facilities = [
  LOG_LOCAL0 => 'LOG_LOCAL0',
  LOG_LOCAL1 => 'LOG_LOCAL1',
  // ... etc
];

If you were relying on the submit handler, use config targets in your form instead:

$form['my_field'] = [
  '#type' => 'textfield',
  '#config_target' => 'my_module.settings:my_key',
];

See #3566774 for details.

Timeline

  • Opened: January 29, 2026
  • Committed: March 1, 2026 (1 month later)

Collaboration

nicxvan opened the issue and drove the refactoring to completion. dcam provided thorough review across multiple iterations, including verifying that no contributed modules actually used these functions despite initial search results suggesting otherwise, and conducting manual testing of the form functionality after each change. longwave suggested further simplification by removing the default value keys since config targets handle that automatically, reducing code even more.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3560260: OEmbedIframeController makes responses uncacheable by mistake
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 867a3c0 · +34/-18 prod · +236/-0 test · 4 files
  • Discussion: #3560260 · 5 contributors · 18 comments

Summary

The oEmbed iframe controller was accidentally preventing responses from being cached due to incorrect cache metadata handling. When displaying remote video embeds from YouTube, Vimeo, and other oEmbed providers, every request would bypass the cache entirely. This caused unnecessary load on external services and slower page performance for visitors. The fix ensures that embedded media responses are now properly cached based on the provider's cache settings, typically improving response times and reducing external API calls.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The OEmbedIframeController::render() method was calling $response->addCacheableDependency(Url::createFromRequest($request)) to vary responses based on the request URL. However, Url does not implement CacheableDependencyInterface, so this silently set the cache max age to 0, making all responses uncacheable.

The fix replaces this with explicit CacheableMetadata that adds the url.site and url cache contexts to vary responses appropriately. The response now properly inherits the cache max age from the oEmbed resource itself (typically set by the provider), capped at 5 years maximum.

Error handling was also improved by switching from BadRequestHttpException to CacheableBadRequestHttpException for validation failures, ensuring that even error responses respect cache metadata. When a ResourceException occurs while fetching the resource, the response max age is explicitly set to 0 to prevent caching errors.

Three new kernel tests verify that the response cache max age matches the resource's cache age, that errors are not cached, and that excessively long cache ages are capped at 5 years.

Timeline

  • Opened: November 27, 2025
  • Committed: March 1, 2026 (3 months later)

Collaboration

arousseau identified the bug and developed the solution through multiple iterations, adding comprehensive test coverage and switching to cacheable exceptions to properly handle error cases. jonhattan contributed to refining the fix. smustgrave provided review feedback that led to using the media.settings config object as a cacheable dependency to automatically invalidate caches when settings change. longwave committed the changes to both main and 11.x branches.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3566782: Deprecate block_theme_initialize() and move the logic to a service
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: ac35794 · +102/-6 · 6 files
  • Discussion: #3566782 · 5 contributors · 24 comments

Summary

Drupal automatically copies block placements from the default theme when a new theme is installed. This behavior is triggered by a procedural function that existed in the block module file. As part of ongoing work to eliminate module files in core, this function has been deprecated and its logic moved into the block module's hook class. The function will be removed in Drupal 13. This change makes it easier to potentially remove this automatic block copying behavior in the future if core maintainers decide it is no longer needed.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: block_theme_initialize() deprecated with no replacement; internal logic moved to protected method in BlockHooks class

Technical details

The block_theme_initialize() function was a procedural function in block.module that copied block placements from the default theme to newly installed themes. This automatic copying behavior dates back to earlier versions of Drupal and has been questioned in related issues like #3105597.

This issue is part of the broader initiative tracked in #3566536 to eliminate .module files from core by moving procedural code into services and hook classes.

The implementation moves the function's logic into a new protected method themeInitialize() in the existing BlockHooks class. The original function remains but now triggers a deprecation error and is marked for removal in Drupal 13.0.0. Internal calls within BlockHooks::modulesInstalled() and BlockHooks::themesInstalled() now call the protected method directly. The API documentation for hook_themes_installed() was updated to remove the example usage of the deprecated function.

Because the replacement method is protected rather than a public service method, there is no direct replacement for external callers. This allows core to maintain the functionality internally while signaling that external code should not rely on it.

Upgrade

If your code calls block_theme_initialize(), remove those calls. There is no direct replacement provided. The function triggered deprecation warnings starting in Drupal 11.4 and will be removed in Drupal 13.0.0.

If you need to initialize blocks for a theme programmatically, you will need to implement your own logic or reconsider whether this is necessary for your use case. The automatic copying of blocks between themes is under evaluation for potential removal.

See the change record at https://www.drupal.org/node/3566783 for details.

Timeline

  • Opened: January 10, 2026
  • Committed: March 1, 2026 (1 month later)

Collaboration

Claudiu Cristea opened this issue as part of the broader effort to eliminate core module files. Berdir raised an important concern about the RTBC queue length and suggested considering the related issue #3105597 which proposes removing this functionality entirely. Nicxvan coordinated with block module maintainer Tim Plunkett to get sign-off on the deprecation approach and confirmed no contrib usage existed. The team decided to proceed with the deprecation without blocking on the removal discussion, as the protected method could be easily dropped later if needed.

Lessons learned

  • When deprecating functionality that may be removed entirely in the future, moving it to a protected method in a service provides flexibility. If the functionality is later deemed unnecessary, a protected method can be removed outright without additional deprecation, whereas a public API requires a full deprecation cycle. (via nicxvan, longwave)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3534144: [12.x] [meta] Set Drupal 12 platform and browser requirements by Jan 15 2026
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical plan
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3534144 · 5 contributors · 32 comments

Summary

Drupal 12 has finalized its platform requirements for hosting environments. The new major version will require PHP 8.5, following the policy to adopt the latest stable PHP release. Database minimums were carefully chosen to balance access to modern features with realistic hosting constraints. Most database versions remain the same or increase conservatively, recognizing that databases are harder to upgrade than PHP in many organizations. These requirements balance supporting newer platform capabilities with making Drupal 12 adoption accessible to a wide range of hosting environments.

Impact

Affects site owners.

  • Requirements: Drupal 12 will require PHP 8.5, MySQL 8.0, MariaDB 10.11, PostgreSQL 18, and SQLite 3.45

Technical details

This meta issue coordinated decisions across multiple subsystems to establish Drupal 12's minimum platform requirements. The PHP requirement follows the recently adopted policy #3406215 to require the highest stable PHP version available at beta release, which will be PHP 8.5.

For databases, the team applied different policies per platform based on their release cycles and ecosystem adoption patterns. MySQL remains at 8.0 despite that version reaching end-of-life before Drupal 12's release, prioritizing easier adoption since the security surface of database-specific features is low. MariaDB increases from 10.6 to 10.11 to follow their LTS lifecycle more closely. PostgreSQL jumps to version 18 based on the policy to require the most recent major version available six months before the Drupal major release. SQLite stays at 3.45 as there were no pressing feature needs.

These decisions considered Ubuntu LTS shipping versions, hosting provider constraints, and the balance between modern capabilities and adoption barriers. The requirements were documented and announced to give hosting providers and site owners advance notice.

Timeline

  • Opened: July 4, 2025

Collaboration

quietone coordinated this meta issue and shepherded it through multiple discussions to reach consensus on each platform requirement. catch and longwave provided detailed analysis of database version policies, weighing release cycles against practical hosting constraints. gábor hojtsy posted the announcement and updated documentation pages to communicate the requirements to the broader community. The team balanced competing concerns between supporting modern platform features and ensuring Drupal 12 remains accessible to a wide range of hosting environments.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3572348: Static cache EntityDataDefinition::getDataType()
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: efe7493 · +34/-0 · 2 files
  • Discussion: #3572348 · 3 contributors · 11 comments

Summary

Batch processing large numbers of entities is now faster. When processing thousands of entities and accessing multiple field values, the system was repeatedly calculating the same data type information. This optimization stores that information after the first calculation instead of recalculating it every time. In one test case processing 4,000 entities, this eliminated 800,000 unnecessary function calls. Sites that process large numbers of entities in batch operations will see performance improvements.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The EntityDataDefinition::getDataType() method constructs a data type string based on entity type and bundle information. This method was being called repeatedly during entity processing, particularly when accessing field values on multiple entities.

The optimization adds a $dataType property that caches the result after the first call. Subsequent calls to getDataType() return the cached value instead of rebuilding the string.

To prevent the cached value from being stored when the object is serialized (such as when stored in cache), a __sleep() method was added that clears the $dataType property before serialization. This keeps serialized objects smaller and ensures the cache is always reconstructed from the source data when needed.

The initial test failure occurred because a test was serializing two objects where only one had called getDataType(), making the serialized versions different. Clearing the property during serialization resolved this.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 9, 2026
  • Committed: March 1, 2026 (19 days later)

Collaboration

catch identified the performance issue through profiling a batch processing scenario and implemented the optimization. longwave committed the change to both the main and 11.x branches.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #2613044: Requests are pushed onto the request stack twice, popped once
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 63d74bd · +78/-60 prod · +492/-10 test · 16 files
  • Discussion: #2613044 · 21 contributors · 150 comments

Summary

Drupal's request handling incorrectly pushed every request onto the request stack twice but only popped it once. This caused duplicate entries during the entire request lifecycle and allowed the stack to grow unbounded across main requests and sub-requests. The fix ensures Drupal manages the request stack lifecycle independently from Symfony's kernel by popping main requests during termination and sub-requests immediately after handling, while letting Symfony use its own internal stack. The request stack is now correctly empty after kernel termination, preventing cache poisoning and state leakage issues.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Symfony's HttpKernel unconditionally pushes and pops requests. Drupal's DrupalKernel::preHandle() also pushed requests early (needed for service initialization during boot), but nothing popped this second push. The double-push caused problems: duplicate stack entries during request processing, sub-requests compounding the issue, and the main request persisting after termination.

The solution decouples Drupal's request stack from Symfony's. http_kernel.basic now receives null for the request stack parameter, causing HttpKernel to create its own internal stack. Drupal maintains its own stack lifecycle: DrupalKernel::preHandle() still pushes early, KernelPreHandle::handle() now pops sub-requests in a finally block after handling, and DrupalKernel::terminate() pops the main request at the very end after destructible services run. This ensures the request remains available throughout Drupal's entire lifecycle including termination events, while the stack is properly cleared afterward. Test coverage validates correct stack depth during main requests, sub-requests, and after termination.

Timeline

  • Opened: November 11, 2015
  • Committed: March 1, 2026 (10 years later)

Collaboration

This issue was open for over 10 years with 150 comments and contributions from 21 people. cpj did extensive research through comments 11-70, methodically testing different architectural approaches including separate request stacks and wrapper classes, before arriving at a working solution. andypost maintained the patch through multiple core versions and API changes. alexpott revived the issue in 2024, simplified the approach to pass null to Symfony's kernel, wrote comprehensive test coverage validating request stack behavior during sub-requests and termination, and shepherded it to completion. larowlan identified a real-world cache poisoning bug caused by this issue that affected production sites.

Lessons learned

  • When integrating with framework components that manage stack-based request lifecycles, different layers may have conflicting expectations about push/pop symmetry. If your application needs resources to remain available longer than the framework's default lifecycle (e.g., during termination handlers), take explicit ownership of the stack lifecycle rather than relying on the framework's automatic cleanup. Use wrapper objects to prevent the framework from modifying shared state while still allowing it to read that state. (via alexpott, cpj)
  • When handling main requests and subrequests in a stack-based architecture, ensure each request type has a clearly scoped push/pop lifecycle. Main requests may need to persist longer than subrequests (e.g., through termination events), while subrequests should be popped immediately after handling. Asymmetric push/pop patterns are acceptable when they accurately reflect the application's requirements, even if they differ from framework conventions. (via alexpott, cpj, dawehner)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #2976416: BaseFieldDefinition::setDefaultValue() and ::setDefaultValueCallback() have no documentation
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 9ba2d1d · +72/-4 prod · +0/-24 infra · 4 files
  • Discussion: #2976416 · 9 contributors · 72 comments

Summary

Two methods for configuring default values on base field definitions now have complete documentation. Previously, BaseFieldDefinition::setDefaultValue() and BaseFieldDefinition::setDefaultValueCallback() used placeholder documentation that referenced nonexistent interface methods, making it difficult for developers to understand how to use them properly. The updated documentation explains the accepted formats for default values, the precedence of callbacks over static values, and the callback signature, helping module developers set up entity base fields correctly.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The BaseFieldDefinition class implements FieldDefinitionInterface, which is intentionally read-only and contains no setter methods. This design reflects that base field definitions are static after discovery, unlike FieldConfig entities which can be modified and saved. The two methods in question inherited {@inheritdoc} tags that pointed to no actual parent, leaving them undocumented.

The fix adds explicit docblocks explaining the accepted value formats for setDefaultValue(): literals (assigned to the first property of the first item), numerically indexed arrays of items, non-numerically indexed property/value arrays, or NULL/empty array. It documents that setDefaultValueCallback() takes precedence when both are set, and specifies the callback signature with two parameters (the entity and field definition) returning a value in the same format.

The type hint for the callback parameter was corrected from string to callable, aligning with PHPStan documentation standards adopted in core. This reflects that while the current implementation accepts string representations of functions, the semantic meaning is that it's a callable. PHPStan baseline entries for missing return type annotations were also removed.

Timeline

  • Opened: May 30, 2018
  • First commit: February 9, 2026 (7 years later)
  • Last commit: February 27, 2026 (17 days later)

Collaboration

joachim opened this issue in 2018 and followed through with detailed reviews over the years. xjm provided thorough committer review focusing on documentation accuracy and consistency with core patterns. oily contributed careful analysis of callable type hinting and PHP semantics that led to improving the parameter documentation from string to callable. quietone committed the changes across multiple branches. The discussion about read-only interfaces versus concrete implementations provided valuable context about entity field definition architecture.

Lessons learned

  • When a parameter accepts a string that represents a callable (function name, class method, or service notation), document it as 'callable' rather than 'string'. This conveys semantic intent and leverages IDE and static analysis tooling that recognizes the callable type. (via oily, xjm)
  • When read-only interfaces describe objects that have mutable implementations, setter methods belong on the concrete class without requiring interface changes. Document these methods explicitly rather than using inheritdoc or forcing setters onto interfaces that semantically represent immutable contracts. (via berdir)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3574461: Deprecate not implementing \Drupal\layout_builder\SupportAwareSectionStorageInterface, implement it in navigation
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: f08dfd4 · +32/-4 prod · +52/-28 test · 12 files
  • Discussion: #3574461 · 3 contributors · 14 comments

Summary

Layout Builder had a performance optimization from #3060985 that prevented unnecessary work when checking if Layout Builder is enabled for an entity. However, the optimization only worked when all section storage plugins properly implemented a support-checking interface. The Navigation module's section storage did not implement this interface, which caused the optimization to fail completely whenever Navigation was enabled. This change adds the interface to Navigation and deprecates section storage plugins that do not implement it, restoring the performance improvements.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: Section storage plugins not implementing \Drupal\layout_builder\SupportAwareSectionStorageInterface is deprecated in drupal:11.4.0 and required from drupal:13.0.0

Technical details

The SectionStorageManager::notSupported() method checks whether Layout Builder should be skipped for a given entity type, bundle, and view mode. A previous optimization introduced SupportAwareSectionStorageInterface to allow section storage plugins to declare whether they support specific contexts without expensive setup work.

The original logic short-circuited if any plugin either didn't implement the interface or returned TRUE from isSupported(). This meant that a single plugin not implementing the interface would cause the optimization to fail for all requests, triggering expensive context resolution unnecessarily.

The fix splits the conditional logic. Now if a plugin doesn't implement the interface, a deprecation error is triggered and the method returns FALSE (not supported), but this only affects that specific check. The Navigation module's NavigationSectionStorage now implements the interface and returns FALSE from isSupported() since it does not support entity-based layouts.

Test section storage plugins (SimpleConfigSectionStorage, TestStateBasedSectionStorage) also received the interface to avoid triggering deprecations during testing. Performance test expectations were updated to reflect the restored optimization, showing fewer queries and cache operations.

Upgrade

If you maintain a custom section storage plugin, implement \Drupal\layout_builder\SupportAwareSectionStorageInterface and add the isSupported() method:

use Drupal\layout_builder\SupportAwareSectionStorageInterface;

class MyCustomSectionStorage extends PluginBase implements SectionStorageInterface, SupportAwareSectionStorageInterface {
  
  public function isSupported(string $entity_type_id, string $bundle, string $view_mode): bool {
    // Return TRUE if this storage applies to the given context.
    // Return FALSE if it does not (e.g., Navigation returns FALSE for all entity types).
    return FALSE;
  }
}

See #3574738 for full details.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 19, 2026
  • Committed: February 27, 2026 (8 days later)

Collaboration

berdir identified the Navigation module as the source of a performance regression and implemented both the fix and the deprecation. catch provided guidance on test coverage expectations and committed the change.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3572339: Modernize locale.fetch.inc
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: b90bfab · +312/-106 · 16 files
  • Discussion: #3572339 · 4 contributors · 15 comments

Summary

The Locale module now provides translation fetching functionality through a proper service instead of procedural functions. This change modernizes the codebase by moving batch building operations for downloading and importing translations into the new LocaleFetch service. Sites using the standard translation update workflows will see no change in behavior. This cleanup is part of an ongoing effort to modernize the Locale module by converting procedural code to object-oriented services.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: Three procedural functions in locale.fetch.inc are deprecated: locale_translation_batch_update_build(), locale_translation_batch_fetch_build(), and _locale_translation_fetch_operations(); use the new LocaleFetch service methods instead.

Technical details

The change introduces a new LocaleFetch service that encapsulates the logic previously in three procedural functions from locale.fetch.inc. The service provides two public methods: batchUpdateBuild() checks translation status then downloads and imports updates, while batchFetchBuild() downloads and imports without checking status first. Both methods return batch definition arrays for the Batch API. A protected fetchOperations() method builds the operation arrays.

The deprecated procedural functions now trigger deprecation errors and delegate to the service. The service uses dependency injection to access LocaleProjectStorageInterface and ModuleExtensionList. All internal Drupal code that called the old functions has been updated to use the service instead, including installation routines, form handlers, cron implementations, and hook implementations.

The _locale_translation_fetch_operations() function was marked internal (leading underscore) and has no public replacement since it was an implementation detail. The file locale.fetch.inc remains for backward compatibility in Drupal 11.x but will be removed in Drupal 13.0.

Upgrade

If your custom code calls the deprecated functions, update to use the LocaleFetch service:

// Before:
$batch = locale_translation_batch_update_build($projects, $langcodes, $options);

// After:
$batch = \Drupal::service(LocaleFetch::class)->batchUpdateBuild($projects, $langcodes, $options);

// Before:
$batch = locale_translation_batch_fetch_build($projects, $langcodes, $options);

// After:
$batch = \Drupal::service(LocaleFetch::class)->batchFetchBuild($projects, $langcodes, $options);

If you called _locale_translation_fetch_operations(), refactor to call one of the two public methods instead. See #3572345 for details.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 9, 2026
  • Committed: February 26, 2026 (17 days later)

Collaboration

nicxvan opened this issue as part of a file-by-file modernization effort to avoid merge conflicts while converting the Locale module. After berdir's feedback about testing installation, nicxvan manually verified the changes. The discussion around keeping implicit includes for backward compatibility in Drupal 11.x involved alexpott and berdir in Slack, establishing a pattern for handling deprecated include files. dcam provided review feedback and confirmed all references were updated before setting the issue back to RTBC.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3575871: Most tests should not try to install obsolete modules
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 87f0787 · +26/-14 · 14 files
  • Discussion: #3575871 · 4 contributors · 12 comments

Summary

Several core tests attempt to install all stable modules to verify functionality like configuration import, REST coverage, and library definitions. These tests were failing when modules were marked as obsolete because the tests only filtered out deprecated modules. This change updates seven test classes to skip obsolete modules in addition to deprecated ones, preventing installation failures and unblocking the removal of obsolete modules from core.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Tests that install all stable modules need to filter out modules that are not suitable for installation. Previously these tests already skipped experimental and deprecated modules, but not obsolete ones. When modules began being marked as ExtensionLifecycle::OBSOLETE, these tests started failing.

The fix adds checks for ExtensionLifecycle::OBSOLETE alongside existing ExtensionLifecycle::DEPRECATED checks in seven test files: ConfigImportAllTest, TestCoverageTest (JSON:API), EntityResourceRestTestCoverageTest (REST), ResolvedLibraryDefinitionsFilesMatchTest, ModuleConfigureRouteTest, Stable9TemplateOverrideTest, and StableLibraryOverrideTestBase.

Each test uses filtering logic to determine which modules to install. The change adds a new condition to exclude modules with the obsolete lifecycle status. This prevents the module installer from attempting to enable modules that are no longer supported, which would cause test failures. This pattern matches the existing approach for deprecated modules, ensuring consistency across lifecycle states.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 26, 2026
  • Committed: February 26, 2026

Collaboration

quietone identified this issue while working on removing Migrate Drupal UI and created the fix. smustgrave reviewed the changes, noting they matched patterns used in the migrate removal ticket. The issue was committed quickly by catch to unblock the broader obsolete module removal work.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3575254: Module deprecation removal part2
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 49c2dc1 · +34/-854 prod · +0/-283 test · +0/-12 infra · 69 files
  • Discussion: #3575254 · 3 contributors · 17 comments

Summary

Drupal 12 cleanup continues by removing deprecated code from core modules. This second phase removes functions, constants, test modules, and routing that were marked for removal in Drupal 12. The changes affect internal code including procedural hooks moved to OOP, deprecated helper functions, unused test infrastructure, and obsolete filter tips pages. No action is needed unless your custom code directly calls deprecated internal functions.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This issue removes deprecations from nine core modules as part of preparing for Drupal 12. The changes include removing deprecated procedural hooks that were converted to OOP (like template_preprocess_block() moved to BlockHooks::preprocessBlock()), eliminating obsolete helper functions (such as _ckeditor5_theme_css() and locale translation utility functions), and removing unused test modules like ckeditor5_icon_deprecation_test and field_ui_test_deprecated.

The issue also removes the filter tips pages and their routes (filter.tips_all and filter.tips), along with FilterController::filterTips(). Deprecated batch operation callbacks in the locale module were removed, and various library file movement declarations were cleaned up. The changes update dozens of template file comments to reference the correct OOP hook implementations instead of removed procedural functions.

Two noteworthy finds during review: a test module that underwent OOP conversion in 2024 but was never actually used by any tests, highlighting the maintenance cost of dead test code. The scope was intentionally limited to keep the merge request reviewable, with additional deprecations left for a future part 3.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 23, 2026
  • Committed: February 26, 2026 (2 days later)

Collaboration

smustgrave opened and shepherded the issue, creating a manageable scope for reviewers. dcam provided thorough review that caught several missed deprecations including zombie test modules, lingering references to deleted functions in docblocks, and a deprecated preprocess function. This type of detailed review prevents technical debt from accumulating. catch merged the changes and noted the scope was appropriate for straightforward removal work, acknowledging that a final sweep will catch any remaining items.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3335756: Drop sequences table in Drupal 12
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 89ac506 · +42/-40 prod · +45/-14 test · 7 files
  • Discussion: #3335756 · 5 contributors · 33 comments

Summary

Drupal 12 completes the removal of the sequences table, which was deprecated in Drupal 10.2. This table was historically used to generate unique IDs but is no longer needed. The installer now checks for the key_value table instead to determine whether Drupal is already installed. Sites upgrading to Drupal 12 will automatically have the sequences table dropped during the update process. Custom or contributed modules that still relied on this table needed to migrate away before Drupal 12 or recreate the table themselves.

Impact

Affects site owners.

  • Database update: The deprecated sequences table is removed by system_update_12000()

Technical details

The sequences table was originally part of the system schema but deprecated in Drupal 10.2. This change fully removes it from Drupal 12.

The system_schema() hook implementation is deleted entirely, as the sequences table was its only remaining definition. The update function system_update_12000() drops the sequences table if it exists.

The installer previously checked for the sequences table (the last table in system_schema()) to determine if Drupal was already installed. This logic is replaced with checks for the key_value table, which is created early in the install process and is a reliable indicator of an existing installation.

The installer's two-phase bootstrap now queries the key_value table directly for the install_task state entry to determine whether a previous partial install exists. This eliminates the dependency on system_schema() while maintaining support for resumed installations.

The change also updates test infrastructure and exception handling to use key_value instead of sequences for detecting database state. Drush already used key_value for this purpose, validating the approach.

Timeline

  • Opened: January 23, 2023
  • Committed: February 26, 2026 (3 years later)

Collaboration

andypost drove the implementation, working through installer bootstrap complications and test coverage. catch provided guidance on early installer behavior and confirmed the approach of using the key_value table check. mstrelan identified the installer dependencies that needed careful handling. dcam requested update path test coverage to ensure existing sites upgrade cleanly.

Lessons learned

  • When validating whether a system is already installed, check for a table that is guaranteed to exist in that system's core operation and cannot be easily swapped out. Relying on deprecated or optional tables leads to false negatives when those tables are removed. (via mstrelan, andypost, catch)
  • When removing deprecated functionality that has been used for system bootstrapping or installation validation, verify that alternative database drivers and service replacements still work. Even if modules can swap implementations, they cannot do so before installation, so core tables are safe to check during early bootstrap. (via catch, andypost)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3450523: Documentation for Navigation module
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3450523 · 6 contributors · 15 comments

Summary

Documentation for the Navigation module was missing when it moved from a contributed module to Drupal core. Site owners and developers needed guidance on what the module does and how to configure its settings. A documentation page has been created at drupal.org that explains the purpose of the Navigation module and its configuration options. This documentation is now available in the standard location for core module documentation, making it easier for site administrators to understand and use the new navigation interface.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Navigation module became part of Drupal core but lacked official documentation at the expected location in the core modules documentation section. Contributors created a new documentation page at https://www.drupal.org/docs/develop/core-modules-and-themes/core-modules/navigation-module to fill this gap.

The documentation was initially based on the change record from #3443691 which explained the module's introduction to core. Contributors expanded this to include configuration guidance and organized the content to group related concepts together. The resulting documentation is now comparable in quality to other core module documentation pages.

This was a documentation-only issue with no code changes to Drupal core itself. The work involved editing the drupal.org website documentation pages rather than making commits to the codebase.

Timeline

  • Opened: May 29, 2024

Collaboration

Prashant.c identified the documentation gap and opened the issue. Catch recognized the issue should have been a stable blocker and suggested using content from the related change record as a foundation. Hestenet helped copy content over when technical issues prevented others from editing the page. Quietone reorganized the documentation to improve its structure and closed the issue, noting that all documentation evolves over time and the community can continue to improve it.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3564735: Container / service exceptions while installed from existing config
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 11.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 24b60a5 · +9/-0 prod · +75/-0 test · 6 files
  • Discussion: #3564735 · 11 contributors · 27 comments

Summary

Installing Drupal from existing configuration could fail with container errors in version 11.3.x. The problem occurred when themes were installed during configuration import, causing the service container to lose track of critical services like the kernel. This manifested as "synthetic service" errors during command-line installations. The fix ensures services are properly reloaded after theme installation, matching the behavior that already existed for modules.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

In Drupal 11.3, #3544715 added OOP hook support to themes. This required the theme installer to rebuild the service container, similar to how the module installer works. However, ConfigImporter::processExtension() was not updated to handle this container rebuild for themes.

When installing modules during config import, the importer calls reInjectMe() to refresh injected services after the container rebuild. This same logic was missing for theme installations. Without it, the ConfigImporter retained references to stale services from the old container, including synthetic services that cannot be reconstructed.

The fix adds a call to $this->reInjectMe() after calling the theme installer service in ConfigImporter::processExtension(), mirroring the existing pattern for modules. This ensures all injected services in the importer are refreshed to reference the newly built container.

The test validates this by checking that the themeHandler property in the importer is replaced with a different object instance after importing a theme, confirming the service was properly reloaded from the rebuilt container.

Timeline

  • Opened: December 24, 2025
  • Committed: February 25, 2026 (2 months later)

Collaboration

kazuko.murata reported the issue with clear reproduction steps and created the initial merge request. geek-merlin refined the issue summary to connect this with the duplicate report and provide architectural context. phenaproxima wrote a test that validates service reloading during theme installation. Multiple contributors including heddn, jeroent, steinmb, and balsama confirmed the fix resolved their installation failures across different environments.

Lessons learned

  • When a subsystem rebuilds the container during a long-running operation (like configuration import), synthetic services become unavailable in the old container reference. After triggering a container rebuild, re-inject services from the new container into the active context so that synthetic services like 'kernel' and stateful services like 'request_stack' remain accessible. (via kazuko.murata, geek-merlin)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #2667040: Deprecate uri_callback in routes for entities
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 8ca8a90 · +140/-42 prod · +6/-0 test · 18 files
  • Discussion: #2667040 · 7 contributors · 48 comments

Summary

Entity types can now specify URLs for viewing, editing, and deleting entities using either link templates or route providers. An older method using uri_callback was still documented and partially functional, but rarely used. This change formally deprecates uri_callback and updates documentation to guide developers toward the modern approaches. The feature will be removed in Drupal 13, giving developers time to migrate any custom entity types still using this legacy pattern.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: The uri_callback property in entity type annotations/attributes is deprecated in Drupal 11.4.0 and removed in Drupal 13.0.0. Use link templates or route providers instead.

Technical details

The uri_callback property allowed entity types to specify a callable that would return routing information for an entity. This approach was largely replaced by link templates and route providers years ago, but the old API remained in place without formal deprecation.

The change adds @deprecated tags to the uri_callback property across all entity type attribute classes (EntityType, ContentEntityType, ConfigEntityType), the EntityType class property, and the getter/setter methods in EntityTypeInterface and EntityType. When uri_callback is set in an attribute constructor or accessed via getUriCallback(), a deprecation notice is triggered.

Documentation in entity.api.php was updated to move uri_callback information to a less prominent position, clearly marking it as deprecated and only used as a fallback when link templates are not provided. The outdated documentation referencing non-existent functions like node_uri() was corrected.

The functionality itself remains unchanged to maintain backward compatibility. The deprecation targets Drupal 13.0.0 for removal, giving custom entity types time to migrate to link templates or route providers.

Upgrade

Custom entity types still using uri_callback should migrate to link templates or a route provider.

For simple cases, replace uri_callback with link templates in your entity type annotation or attribute:

// Old (deprecated):
uri_callback = "my_entity_uri",

// New:
links = {
  "canonical" = "/my-entity/{my_entity}",
  "edit-form" = "/my-entity/{my_entity}/edit",
  "delete-form" = "/my-entity/{my_entity}/delete",
}

For more complex routing needs, implement a route provider by specifying a handlers entry and creating a class that implements EntityRouteProviderInterface.

See the change record at https://www.drupal.org/node/3575062 for complete migration guidance.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 11, 2016
  • Committed: February 25, 2026 (10 years later)

Collaboration

This issue was opened by eojthebrave in 2016 when they discovered outdated documentation referencing removed APIs. After years of dormancy, longwave picked it up during a bug smash triage session, recognizing it as a deprecation opportunity. They moved the work to a merge request and added comprehensive deprecation notices across all affected classes. godotislate provided valuable review feedback, catching a missing documentation reference and ensuring the change record was created and linked properly. The issue demonstrates the project's commitment to cleaning up legacy APIs even when they've been unofficially deprecated for years.

Lessons learned

  • When removing or changing a feature that developers configure through annotations or attributes, add deprecation warnings at the point where the configuration is consumed, not just in property definitions. Detecting deprecated configuration during entity info collection ensures developers receive actionable feedback. (via catch)
  • When deprecating entity configuration options like uri_callback, direct developers to both the consumer API (toUrl()) and the provider pattern (link templates or route handlers). Developers creating entities need guidance on how to structure their entity type, not just how to consume it. (via jhodgdon, longwave)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3558306: Support automatic setter injection using the #[Required] attribute in AutowireTrait/AutowiredInstanceTrait
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 4041964 · +102/-32 prod · +118/-4 test · 8 files
  • Discussion: #3558306 · 4 contributors · 25 comments

Summary

Controllers and plugins can now inject additional services using setter methods marked with the #[Required] attribute instead of extending parent constructors. This makes it easier for child classes to add their own dependencies when the parent class already has a long constructor signature. The setter methods are automatically called during object creation, similar to how Symfony handles autowired service definitions. This reduces boilerplate code and makes dependency management more flexible for complex class hierarchies.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: Controllers and plugins can now use the #[Required] attribute on public setter methods to automatically inject services without extending constructors.

Technical details

The AutowiredInstanceTrait::createInstanceAutowired() method now scans for public methods with the #[Required] attribute after instantiating the object. It automatically resolves and injects parameters into these setter methods using the same autowiring logic as the constructor.

The implementation refactored the existing autowiring logic into a new getAutowireArguments() helper method that both the constructor and setter injection paths share. This method resolves service parameters by type hint or #[Autowire] attribute, handles nullable parameters, and throws AutowiringFailedException when resolution fails.

After the instance is created with constructor arguments, reflection iterates through all public methods. For each method with the #[Required] attribute, it invokes the method with autowired arguments. This matches Symfony's service definition behavior but applies it to ContainerInjectionInterface and ContainerFactoryPluginInterface classes.

The exception message was improved to provide better guidance when autowiring fails, suggesting that developers check their type hints or #[Autowire] attributes. Test coverage was added via AutowiredInstanceTraitTest to verify setter injection works correctly.

Upgrade

Classes using AutowireTrait or AutowiredInstanceTrait can now add public setter methods with the #[Required] attribute to inject services:

use Symfony\Contracts\Service\Attribute\Required;

class ExamplePlugin extends PluginBase {

  protected MyServiceInterface $myService;

  #[Required]
  public function setMyService(MyServiceInterface $myService): void {
    $this->myService = $myService;
  }
}

Multiple setter methods can be used, or one setter can accept multiple parameters. The #[Autowire] attribute can be used on parameters if needed. Setter methods are called automatically during instantiation when using the autowiring system.

Timeline

  • Opened: November 17, 2025
  • Committed: February 23, 2026 (3 months later)

Collaboration

longwave implemented the feature with help from Google Gemini, manually reviewing and refining the AI-generated code. godotislate opened the issue and provided detailed examples of the use case, then contributed refinements to exception messages and documentation. claudiu.cristea provided thoughtful feedback on method chaining patterns and documentation clarity, ensuring the change record examples covered common scenarios.

Lessons learned

  • When extending a class with autowired constructor dependencies, use setter injection with the #[Required] attribute instead of adding constructor parameters. This avoids needing to redeclare all parent constructor parameters just to add one subclass-specific dependency. (via godotislate, longwave)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3575066: Olivero mobile close button not properly placed against top nav bar
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 66e0d15 · +42/-0 · 6 files
  • Discussion: #3575066 · 4 contributors · 12 comments

Summary

When using the Olivero theme on mobile devices with the Navigation module enabled, the close button for the mobile menu overlapped with the search bar, making it difficult to use either element. This fix adds padding to the mobile navigation panel when the Navigation module's top bar is present, ensuring the close button and search bar no longer overlap and remain properly accessible.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The issue occurred when the Navigation module was enabled alongside the Olivero theme on mobile viewports. The mobile navigation panel's close button would overlay the search bar, creating a usability problem.

The fix adds a CSS rule to header-navigation.css and header-navigation.pcss.css that targets the mobile navigation panel when the Navigation module's control bar is present. Using the :has() selector to detect body:has(> .admin-toolbar-control-bar), the rule adds padding-top: 3rem (48px) to the navigation panel on viewports narrower than 63.9375rem (1023px). This pushes the navigation content down, preventing overlap with the mobile top bar elements.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 22, 2026
  • Committed: February 22, 2026

Collaboration

byfransheska@gmail.com identified and reported the issue with clear steps to reproduce. lauriii reviewed and committed the fix, making a small refinement to the code comment for clarity before merging.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571623: Remove deprecated code from the Node module
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 637e0e3 · +47/-684 prod · +3/-180 test · 41 files
  • Discussion: #3571623 · 6 contributors · 22 comments

Summary

Drupal 12 deprecated code has been removed from the Node module. This cleanup removes functions, classes, and hooks that were deprecated during the Drupal 11 cycle. The most visible changes include the removal of the deprecated Syndicate block, the node_storage_body_field module, and several procedural functions that have been replaced with object-oriented alternatives. The removal also includes deprecated template variables and the old hook_ranking() in favor of hook_node_search_ranking(). This is internal cleanup work that should not affect sites that have already updated their code to avoid deprecation warnings.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This issue removes all Drupal 12 deprecated code from the Node module except for node_mark(), which will be removed separately when the History module is removed. The changes span several areas of the Node module's API and implementation.

Major removals include the node_storage_body_field module, which was a backwards compatibility shim, and the Syndicate block (SyndicateBlock), which provided an RSS feed link. Deprecated procedural functions like node_title_list(), node_type_get_description(), node_add_body_field(), node_access_view_all_nodes(), and node_reindex_node_search() have been removed. The template_preprocess_node() and template_preprocess_node_add_list() functions were removed as their logic was moved to NodeThemeHooks service methods.

The NodeStorage::updateType() method was removed without replacement. The NodePreviewController::title() callback was removed as the title is now generated through standard entity label mechanisms. Required constructor parameters were added to several classes including NodeAccessGrantsCacheContext, NodePreviewController, NodeGrantDatabaseStorage, NodePermissions, and NodeViewsData where previously they were optional with deprecation warnings.

The diff also removes node.admin.inc entirely, moving the remaining node_mass_update() function to node.module where it remains deprecated until Drupal 13. Template variables teaser and metadata were removed from node templates. Library definitions for moved JavaScript files were cleaned up. References to the old hook_ranking() were updated to reference hook_node_search_ranking() throughout documentation.

Timeline

  • Opened: February 5, 2026
  • Committed: February 22, 2026 (17 days later)

Collaboration

dcam opened the issue and coordinated the scope of removals. longwave created the initial merge request and identified edge cases like the node.admin.inc file deprecation timing mismatch. andypost expanded the cleanup to include the node_storage_body_field module and migration-related deprecations, working through test failures to ensure removals were complete. The team coordinated to preserve migration plugins that need to be removed together in a separate effort.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3562072: Menu link content export with dependencies doesn't include parent menu link entity.
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 54bc930 · +255/-0 prod · +285/-0 test · 9 files
  • Discussion: #3562072 · 4 contributors · 17 comments

Summary

When using the default content export system to export menu links that have parent menu links, the parent menu links were not being included as dependencies. This meant that exported menu hierarchies would be incomplete, making it difficult to reliably move menu structures between sites. This bug fix ensures that when you export a child menu link with the --with-dependencies flag, the parent menu link is automatically included in the export.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The default content export system provides a PreExportEvent that allows modules to add additional dependencies when entities are exported. The menu_link_content module was not subscribing to this event, so parent menu link relationships were not being tracked during export.

The fix adds a new DefaultContentSubscriber event subscriber to the menu_link_content module. The subscriber's preExport() method checks if the entity being exported is a MenuLinkContentInterface instance and whether it has a parent menu link (identified by the menu_link_content: prefix in the parent ID). If a parent exists, it loads the parent entity using EntityRepositoryInterface::loadEntityByUuid() and adds it as a dependency via $event->metadata->addDependency().

If the parent cannot be loaded (for example, if it was deleted), the subscriber logs an error to the default_content logger channel with the UUIDs of both the parent and child menu links. The service is registered in menu_link_content.services.yml with autowiring and autoconfiguration enabled.

Timeline

  • Opened: December 8, 2025
  • Committed: February 20, 2026 (2 months later)

Collaboration

vishalkhode reported this issue and provided the initial implementation. phenaproxima provided thorough review feedback and converted the test from a functional test to a more maintainable kernel test, making it easier to verify the fix. godotislate reviewed the changes and confirmed the test-only failure worked as expected. alexpott committed the fix to three branches.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3443571: Mobile version of Navigation should have focus trap
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 6aaf45e · +72/-2 prod · +616/-6 test · 9 files
  • Discussion: #3443571 · 14 contributors · 91 comments

Summary

The Navigation module now prevents keyboard users from accidentally focusing on content hidden behind the mobile sidebar. When the sidebar is open on narrow screens, the rest of the page becomes inert so keyboard navigation stays within the visible menu. This fixes an accessibility issue where tabbing through the menu would move focus to elements obscured by the sidebar, violating WCAG 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured guidelines. The focus trap only applies at mobile widths and removes itself when the sidebar closes or the viewport widens.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Navigation module's mobile sidebar now implements a focus trap using the HTML inert attribute. When the sidebar opens at widths below 1024px, a new toggleFocusTrap() function in admin-toolbar-wrapper.js adds the inert attribute to all direct children of body except the sidebar and overlay. This prevents keyboard and screen reader users from accessing hidden content.

The implementation listens for two events. The sidebar's toggle event calls toggleFocusTrap() when opening or closing on mobile. A matchMedia listener on the viewport width removes inert attributes when resizing to desktop and reapplies them when shrinking back to mobile if the sidebar is open.

Elements with inert cannot receive focus, be clicked, or appear in the accessibility tree. The function also adds a data-admin-toolbar-inert attribute for tracking which elements were made inert.

Three new Nightwatch tests verify the behavior. The tests check that inert attributes are added on mobile when the sidebar opens, removed when it closes, and never applied at desktop widths. The tests also verify that overlay clicks properly close the sidebar and remove the focus trap.

Timeline

  • Opened: April 25, 2024
  • Committed: February 18, 2026 (1 year later)

Collaboration

Mike Herchel opened the issue in April 2024 and drove it through a long accessibility review process. He implemented the inert-based solution after exploring alternatives, wrote comprehensive Nightwatch tests using AI assistance, and responded to testing feedback from multiple reviewers over many months.

Kent Richards improved the issue summary, created two followup issues for the close button and ESC key behavior, and kept the scope focused on the core fix. Roland Koller uncovered browser caching issues during testing that initially made the fix appear broken, persisting until the root cause was identified. Catch suggested the cache directory cleanup that resolved Koller's testing problems.

Lessons learned

  • When implementing modal-like UI components that overlay page content, use the inert attribute on background elements to prevent keyboard navigation and screen reader access to obscured content. This ensures keyboard focus cannot reach elements hidden behind the overlay, satisfying accessibility requirements without manually trapping tab events or preventing access to browser UI. (via mherchel, rkoller)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.
